articles.title: What Is Managed Identity?
articles.summary:
**Managed Identity** is an Azure feature that allows an **Azure resource** to securely authenticate with other Azure services **without storing usernames, passwords, or secrets**.

Instead of keeping credentials in application configuration or source code, Azure automatically creates and manages an identity for the application. The application can then use this identity to access services such as **Azure Key Vault**, **Azure Storage**, or **Azure SQL Database**.

Managed Identity simplifies authentication while improving the **security** and **maintainability** of cloud applications.

content.text: Why use Managed Identity?
content.markdown: ## Why use Managed Identity?

content.text:
Applications often need to access other Azure services.

For example, an **ASP.NET Core** application hosted in **Azure App Service** may need to retrieve a connection string from **Azure Key Vault**.

Without Managed Identity, the application would need to store credentials somewhere, increasing the risk of exposing sensitive information.

Managed Identity eliminates this problem by allowing Azure to authenticate the application automatically.
content.markdown:
Applications often need to access other Azure services.

For example, an **ASP.NET Core** application hosted in **Azure App Service** may need to retrieve a connection string from **Azure Key Vault**.

Without Managed Identity, the application would need to store credentials somewhere, increasing the risk of exposing sensitive information.

Managed Identity eliminates this problem by allowing Azure to authenticate the application automatically.

content.text: How does it work?
content.markdown: ## How does it work?

content.text:
When **Managed Identity** is enabled for an Azure resource, Azure creates an identity for that resource.

When the application requests access to another Azure service, Azure verifies the identity and issues a temporary access token.

If the identity has the required permissions, the requested resource is returned without the application providing a username or password.

    component "Azure Instance\nMetadata Service (IMDS)" as IMDS

    component "Microsoft Entra ID" as Entra

    component "Azure Key Vault" as KeyVault

}

App --> IMDS : Request access token

IMDS --> Entra : Verify Managed Identity

IMDS --> App : Access token

App --> KeyVault : Read secret\n(Bearer Token)

KeyVault --> App : Secret

articles.title: What is continuous integration ?
articles.summary: **Continuous Integration** (CI) is a software development practice (process) where code changes are automatically built, tested, and integrated into the main code base frequently.

articles.title: What is continuous deployment ?
articles.summary: **Continuous Deployment** (CD) is a software development practice (process) of automatically deploying code changes to production or staging environments after passing through the CI pipeline.

articles.title: What is GitHub Actions ?
articles.summary: **GitHub Actions** is a CI/CD automation platform built into GitHub. It allows you to automatically build, test, validate, and deploy your application whenever code is pushed or a pull request is created.

articles.title: What is GitHub Actions workflow file ?
articles.summary:
**GitHub Actions workflow file** is a YAML config file that defines an automated CI/CD pipeline.

It specifies when the pipeline should run and what steps should be executed.

Workflow files are stored in `.github/workflows/` directory, eg. `.github/workflows/ci.yml`

articles.title: How to create GitHub Actions workflow file ?
articles.summary:
Open `Actions` tab in your GitHub repository and click `New workflow` button.

`Set up a workflow yourself` or use one of the predefined templates.

Alternatively create `.github/workflows.ci.yaml` file manually and commit.

articles.title: What are the components of GitHub Actions workflow file ?
articles.summary:
A GitHub Actions workflow file consists of **triggers**, **jobs**, and **steps**.

**Triggers** define when the workflow runs.

**Jobs** define the tasks to perform (in parallel by default).

**Steps** define the individual commands, actions or scripts to be executed.

name: build-test-deploy

on:
  push:
    branches:
      - main

env:

  DOTNET_VERSION: '10.0.x'
  AZURE_WEBAPP_NAME: build-wise
  PROJECT_PATH: src/BuildWise.Web/BuildWise.Web.csproj
  PUBLISH_DIR: publish

jobs:

  build-test-deploy:

    environment: Production
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:

      - name: Checkout repository
        uses: actions/checkout@v6

      - name: Setup .NET
        uses: actions/setup-dotnet@v4
        with:
          dotnet-version: ${{ env.DOTNET_VERSION }}

      - name: Restore
        run: dotnet restore

      - name: Build
        run: dotnet build --configuration Release --no-restore

      - name: Test
        run: dotnet test --configuration Release --no-build

      - name: Publish
        run: |
          dotnet publish \
            ${{ env.PROJECT_PATH }} \
            --configuration Release \
            --no-build \
            --output ${{ env.PUBLISH_DIR }}

      - name: Deploy to Azure App Service
        uses: azure/webapps-deploy@v3
        with:
          app-name: ${{ env.AZURE_WEBAPP_NAME }}
          publish-profile: ${{ secrets.AZURE_WEBAPP_PUBLISH_PROFILE }}
          package: ${{ env.PUBLISH_DIR }}

      - name: TEST COMMANDS
        run: |
          printf "\n========================================\n"
          printf "pwd\n"
          printf "========================================\n\n"

          pwd

          printf "\n========================================\n"
          printf "ls\n"

articles.title: What is Infrastructure as Code ?
articles.summary:
**Infrastructure as Code (IaC)** is the practice of provisioning and managing infrastructure through software (code) to achieve **consistent** and **predictable** environments.

Instead of manually creating resources through a portal, infrastructure is defined as code and deployed automatically.


Popular Infrastructure as Code tools include:

- **Terraform**
- **Azure Bicep**
- ARM Templates

content.summary:
Infrastructure as Code helps organizations:

- Create **consistent environments**
- Create **predictable deployments**
- Reduce manual errors
- Automate infrastructure provisioning

Common resources managed with IaC include:

- Virtual Machines
- Networks
- Databases
- Storage Accounts
- Kubernetes Clusters
Infrastructure as Code is a fundamental practice in modern cloud and DevOps environments.

articles.title: Core Concepts of Infrastructure as Code
articles.summary:
**Infrastructure as Code** is based on several important concepts:

- Infrastructure is defined as **code**
- Infrastructure is stored in **source control**
- Infrastructure can be **declarative** or imperative
- Infrastructure should be **idempotent** and **consistent**

articles.title: What is Terraform?
articles.summary:
**Terraform** is an **Infrastructure as Code (IaC)** tool created by **HashiCorp**.



Key Characteristics of Terraform

- **Platform agnostic** (Azure, AWS, GCP)
- Uses a **declarative** syntax
- Uses **HCL (HashiCorp Configuration Language)** or JSON
- Distributed as a **single binary**
- Does not require a **central server** or **agent**
- Runs directly from the terminal

articles.title: Terraform Versions
articles.summary:
Terraform is available in three editions:

* **Terraform Community Edition** – free and open Infrastructure as Code tool
* **HCP Terraform** – cloud-hosted Terraform service managed by HashiCorp (SaaS)
* **Terraform Enterprise** – self-hosted enterprise version of HCP Terraform

All editions use the same core Terraform concepts and workflows.

articles.title: Terraform Core Components
articles.summary:
Terraform consists of four core components:

- **Terraform Binary** – the executable that contains the core Terraform functionality.
- **Configuration Files** – infrastructure definitions written in **HCL** and stored in `.tf` files.
- **Provider Plugins** – components that allow Terraform to communicate with platform APIs such as **Azure**, **AWS**, **GCP**.
- **State Data** – information about the current infrastructure used by Terraform to compare the **desired configuration** with the **existing environment** and determine required changes.

articles.title: Terraform Workflow
articles.summary:
Terraform uses three main phases:

- **Write** – define infrastructure as code
- **Plan** – compare configuration with state and generate an **execution plan**
- **Apply** – apply changes and update **state data**

articles.title: Installing Terraform
articles.summary:
Terraform installation is simple:

- Download the **Terraform binary** for your operating system
- Add it to your **PATH environment variable**
- Start using Terraform

Terraform can be installed on:

- Windows
- Linux
- macOS

Installation methods include:

- **Binary download**
- **Package managers**
- **Docker**

After installation, Terraform can be accessed from the **command line**.

articles.title: Terraform CLI Basics
articles.summary:
Terraform is primarily used through the **Command Line Interface (CLI)**.

Terraform commands follow this format --> `terraform <command> [options]`

Useful commands include:

- `terraform version`
- `terraform -h`
- `terraform plan -h`

The help system provides information about available commands and options.

articles.title: Main Terraform Commands
articles.summary:
Terraform provides several core commands used to manage infrastructure:

- `init` → prepare the working directory for Terraform
- `validate` → validate the configuration files
- `plan` → generate an **execution plan** showing proposed changes
- `apply` → create or update **infrastructure**
- `destroy` → remove previously created **infrastructure**

articles.title: Terraform Configuration Files
articles.summary:
**Terraform configurations** are created from all **`.tf`** and **`.tf.json`** files located in the **current working directory**.

Terraform combines these files into a single **configuration** when commands such as **`terraform init`** or **`terraform plan`** are executed.

**`.tf` files** use **HashiCorp Configuration Language (HCL)**, while **`.tf.json` files** use **JSON**.

**HCL** is the preferred format because it is more **human-readable**, supports **comments**, and allows the use of **functions** and **complex expressions**.

articles.title: VS Code Extensions for Terraform
articles.summary:
**Visual Studio Code** provides several extensions that improve the Terraform development experience.

Recommended extensions include:

- **HashiCorp Terraform** → syntax highlighting, IntelliSense, formatting
- **TFLint** → linting and validation
- **Error Lens** → inline error display
- **GitHub Actions** → CI/CD workflow support
- **YAML (Red Hat)** → YAML editing support
- **GitLens** → Git integration and history
- **Terraform Docs** → module documentation generation
- **Trivy / tfsec** → infrastructure security scanning

articles.title: Terraform Objects and Blocks
articles.summary:
Terraform configurations are built from **blocks** written in **HashiCorp Configuration Language (HCL)**.

A block contains:

- A **block type**
- One or more **labels**
- **Arguments** (key-value pairs)
- Optional **nested blocks**

Each **resource type + name label** combination must be **unique** within a Terraform configuration.
